api: PyTorch XPU + SYCL + Level Zero IPC

tests.name: single_kernel_write_spin_read
tests.symptom: Some ranks read stale sentinel/old peer values; late-launching rank often succeeds.
tests.interpretation: Cross-device ordering/visibility inside one kernel is not solved by a host barrier before launch.

tests.name: two_phase_mailbox_write_then_peer_read_reduce
tests.output_summary: All four ranks printed ok=True with qk_var rows [2.5, 25.0].

tests.name: single_kernel_sequence_counter_write_poll_reduce
tests.output_summary: All four ranks printed ok=True at iteration 49 with qk_var rows [51.5, 515.0].
tests.caveat: The earlier 500M spin timeout made failures or slow visibility look like hangs during experiments; the timeout is now tunable.

tests.name: device_counter_sequence_reused_slots
tests.output_summary: All four ranks printed ok=True at iteration 49 while reusing three mailbox slots.
tests.interpretation: Device-side sequence counters avoid Python integer sequence constants and are the preferred candidate for compiled vLLM integration.

tests.name: device_counter_sequence_prefill_sized_payload
tests.output_summary: All four ranks printed ok=True at iteration 9 for 512 token rows with three reused slots.
tests.interpretation: The mailbox protocol is not limited to decode-sized one-token payloads, though runtime was much longer than the 32-row stress.

tests.name: scalar_sequence_reused_slots
tests.output_summary: All four ranks printed ok=True at iteration 49 while reusing three mailbox slots.
tests.interpretation: The scalar sequence marker is correct in the standalone harness, but it later proved much too slow inside vLLM.

tests.name: scalar_sequence_prefill_sized_payload
tests.output_summary: All four ranks printed ok=True at iteration 9 for 512 token rows with three reused slots.
tests.interpretation: Correctness still holds for a prefill-sized payload, but standalone correctness did not translate to vLLM throughput.

tests.name: device_counter_no_host_barrier_decode_correctness
tests.output_summary: All four ranks printed ok=True at iteration 49 without a host barrier.
tests.interpretation: The sequence protocol can make progress without a Python barrier when mailbox slots are not reused too early.

tests.name: device_counter_no_host_barrier_slot_wrap
tests.interpretation: With no host barrier and too few slots, a faster rank can overwrite a slot before a slower rank observes the expected sequence. Future mailbox designs need per-layer ring slots or a protocol that cannot miss sequence values.

tests.name: device_counter_decode_microbench_no_barrier
tests.interpretation: Orders of magnitude slower than the XCCL tiny-allreduce microbenchmark; not viable as standalone oneCCL replacement.

tests.name: device_counter_decode_microbench_with_barrier
tests.interpretation: Host launch alignment does not materially change the cost; remote polling/system-scope fencing is the likely bottleneck.

decision: The B70 stack supports PyTorch tensor Level Zero IPC handles and SYCL peer mailbox reads across four worker processes. Device-counter and scalar-sequence mailbox prototypes are correct under controlled stress, but the current mailbox allreduce is far too slow for decode-sized Q/K variance and unsafe under slot wrap without a larger ring. Future IPC work should only be revisited as part of a larger fused kernel.
